>immigrants are low skilled

Legal or illegal, for clarification purposes? And are you claiming that democrats would be more against illegal immigration, or be tougher on immigration itself?

So the idea that only well-off "yuppies" are supporting these immigration policies is false. The majority of low-income people are democrats, and therefore would be fighting in the same competitive market you've described.

>  If everyone identified completely with the opposite sex, how would we conceive the next generation?

There is a hugely problematic gap in this logic. First, in this context, identifying as a gender does not necessarily equate with identifying with (or changing) biological sex. Next, sex and gender do not imply sexual orientation or, for that matter, family planning. And even if all these things did fit neatly into some semblance of binary rules--which they absolutely do not and never will--there is zero reason to believe that a wider acceptance of people's sexual proclivities will lead to some mass exodus of heterosexual breeding. 

But perhaps most importantly, your statement hypothetically assumes a scenario that has as close as you can get to a 0% chance of happening, in order to support your distaste for an extreme minority of the population. It would be like saying that childless couples are unreasonable because if everyone were childless humanity would die off. (Actually, that would be even more generous than your statement, since there are many more childless couples than transgender people.) Yeah, in an impossible analogy that you create for purely rhetorical reasons, sure. Now let's get back to reality.

Treating a disorder shouldn't mean succumbing to it. However, the American Psychological Association has determined that being transgender is not a mental disorder, but being transabled is. [Allowing people to be comfortable with the gender identity that their brains physically match] (https://www.scientificamerican.com/article/is-there-something-unique-about-the-transgender-brain/) is good for their psychological well-being. The desire to maim oneself unnecessarily is not good for one's own well-being and is thus considered a mental disorder. 

>Why is it healthy and moral to encourage HRT and SRS? 

To answer you question it's because years and years of scientific research and use of these treatments have proven them to be successful in helping create a healthier individual.

@horizontal-color @collinear-coulomb So, are we just supposed to ignore the fact that this whole debate seems to be ignoring actual biological realities? I mean, it’s all well and good to talk about mental health and comfort, but at the end of the day, can we really pretend biology doesn’t matter at all? Just asking...

@skinny-statement But why does biology have to be the ultimate decider here? Isn't part of human progress about challenging what we think is 'natural' or 'biological'? Plus, if we're talking about making people happy and mentally healthy, shouldn't that count for something too? Just saying, maybe we're looking at this all wrong.

Convenient how you left the part out where each child who grows up to be an adult will add over 1 million to the economy MINIMUM. 

And if they get a doctorate, they'll add 4 million each. Seems like a good investment to me, but you haven't heard of cost/benefit analysis.

It's tough to find good numbers for this, but in my quick search it looks like around a million abortions occur in the U.S. each year. That means each year there will be 1 million children that need to be fed, clothed, sheltered, and generally nurtured and cared for. It costs around $300,000 to raise a child to the age of 18. Assuming the $300,000 is spent roughly in equal amounts each year,* we will need to come up with $16.6 billion the first year in order to pay for the children. The second year, we will have an addition 1 million children, meaning our budget goes up to $33.3 billion. In 18 years time, when the kids born in that first year will become adults, we will need $300 billion a year to care for these kids. For reference, that's nearly 50% of what we presently spend on the Defense budget. Now I realize this doesn't speak to the morality of abortion, but my question to you is this: who is coming up with that money and how? 

^(*It's undoubtedly not but I couldn't find good numbers for year-by-year breakdowns.)

I’m by no means an expert but thought I would throw my 2 cents in. 

I have lived in both the US and Canada. Though I much prefer the Canadian healthcare system, I also lean toward a secondary system put in place. 

Example: my grandmother recently had surgery to realign a toe. To have the toe operated on within the Canadian Healthcare system, she was looking at an eight month wait. It didn’t seem outrageous as it wasn’t an emergency. 

However, my grandmother didn’t want to wait eight months. She’d made up her mind. So she had the operation done privately, choosing to pay out of pocket. 

I don’t know about the rest of Canada, but in Quebec the option is almost always there to have something done privately. If you have additional healthcare coverage through a company or such, most of the time it will cover part of the private care as well. 

This blended process ensures that everyone gets the care they need, regardless of their economic status, but also allows those who can afford it to “skip the line”. 

This alleviates the “downside” that you mentioned.

@inclusive-gofer So basically you're saying the rich get to cut in line while grandma waits 8 months? Sounds fair... not. How's that working out for the folks who can't afford to "skip the line"? Just curious.

I wouldn't really say it's the same kind oppression as PoC. Every minority group is treated differently. It's not a contest to see which group is treated the worst.

But I have seen plenty of threads here on reddit mocking vegans. And the vitriol stems from a similar thing: it's an underrepresented group being talked about in a public setting. If no one sticks up for them, they'll be treated unfairly.

But please don't think I'm trying to say "Vegans get treated as bad as people of color." because that's not the point I'm trying to convey and isn't what I think.

Many of the social justice issues involve groups that are a small minority of the population. Since this group is such a small minority, their opinions are often not heard in social discourse, for two reasons. The first is obvious: there's not many of them. The second is that they're an outgroup, and often people specifically ignore or dislike people in other groups ([well studied phenomenon](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/In-group_favoritism)).

As such, when a minority group aces an issue, be it a special need (perhaps wheelchair accessable entrances), or just being treated fairly by others (for example segregated schools), it is often dismissed in public discourse. This results in these minority groups having a poorer quality of life, and it sucks overall.

"Social Justice Warriors" recognize this. They realize that if they leave the minority group to fend for themselves (as in "they should worry less about issues that shouldn't concern them"), minority interests won't be fairly represented. So instead of saying it's "somebody else's problem", they say it's "society's problem". And we should work together to fix it.

Also, that's not to say there is no self interest involved. There's a [famous quote](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/First_they_came_...) about the holocaust that ends "Then they came for me—and there was no one left to speak for me." Almost everybody is apart of some kind of minority group. Whether it's veganism, being amish, darker skin, or polyamorous, everybody's got something that sets them apart from most other people. So they hope that by sticking up for other minority groups when they need it, others will stick up for them when their group is in need.

Finally, it's not exclusive. While yes, it's possible to focus on social justice issues and use that as an excuse to not better yourself, it doesn't *have* to be that way. You can spend most of your time improving your own condition, and still find a bit of time leftover to stick up for the little guy.

The point of giving these communities special protections from normal laws wasn't to fix their social problems, it was to finally return the autonomy that was taken away from them for so many years.

Remember, some of the worst offenses that were made against these groups were in the name of "helping" them and making them more "civilized".  

You may very well have their best interests at heart and may in fact be able to improve their situation (albeit, according to our standards), but only by once again removing their autonomy.

In other words, they may be making the wrong choices but at least they are making their own choices.  After all, if they really want to exchange their autonomy for help, they can always reject their status and join the rest of Canadian society.

message:
> their targeted ID laws, 

I always have to ask whenever someone talks about ID laws, what else should not need an ID. You have a right to a gun as much as you have the right to a vote in this country, yet you need a photo ID to buy a gun. What about alcohol/tobacco? Many places card under 35, some places always card, my 80+ year old grandmother has been carded as a matter of policy. Should you need photo ID to get various welfare programs such as food stamps as you currently need? What about a photo ID to adopt a dog from a state run shelter in some states?

Also, the poor can get a photo ID, you can even get a photo ID through the mail. I know because I have one. you can even get a waiver for fees if you are poor. 

You want an ID through the mail? [Here you go](http://www.vsp.state.va.us/Firearms_NonresidentConcealed.shtm) I got mine in 120 days or less. 

Also isn't it possible you are creating a bit of a straw man? 

>Republicans do not care for democratic norms and are willing to use government to oppress minorities and 'others' they view as inferior or dangerous to achieve their ends. 

Thats a little silly, you don't honestly believe that the goal is just hate for the sake of blind hate do you? Its like how the democrats treat straight white guys as "others" it doesn't mean that they hate them, but that isn't their voting base. message:
Sure, you can point out flaws, but it's not really about the flaws. These are the exact same flaws that don't matter to a person when they like a movie. Do you consider the original Star Wars movies good? Because they're no less riddled with plot holes, spotty acting, and improbable coincidences, plus the plot is every bit as derivative. Even as someone who loved The Force Awakens, I could easily point out ten more things wrong with the movie off the top of my head, because these kinds of flaws only get brought up as a post-hoc rationalization of how someone already feels about a movie.

> So Falcon can jump from lightspeed past a shield, but no other ships can? Why don't all the rebel ships do this? Is this tactic completely unknown to the first order?

Anyone can try, but it's suicidally dangerous and takes serious skill. skill. It's in line with what we already know about Han Solo from previous movies that he's an extraordinary pilot and willing to take that kind of insane risk.

> How do Rey and Finn leave Tatooine v2.0 without the Star Destroyer following them? Why only send 2 TIE fighters after Finn? How does the Falcon start up instantly after being abandoned for a long time? Where does that pilot guy go? Why didn't he look for his droid? Is there anything Rey isn't amazing at after just 30 seconds of trying? Why are there only a dozen troopers guarding the Death Star v3.0 weak spot? Why is every other character comic relief? What is wrong with Han's blaster? Has he really never fired Chewie's bowcaster ever? How does R2D2 know to suddenly wake up? Why is Carrie Fisher such a terrible actress? Why doesn't her face move? How is Rey so pale despite living on a desert planet her entire life?

Finn and Rey can leave Jakku without being followed because they have the fastest ship in the galaxy. The Falcon starts up right away as opposed to taking how long, exactly? What's our baseline in this futuristic universe for how long it should take? The pilot guy explained what happened to him and couldn't look for his droid because someone else got to it first. I can keep going but I don't think it matters. If I can give you a good answer to every one of these questions, does your opinion of the movie change, or am I right to assume that it's not really contingent on any of those details? If you don't like The Force Awakens, that's a valid opinion. But these are the kinds of nitpicks that most people can point out even in their favorite movies.

Classic plot devices, right? 😂 But seriously, if we’re gonna nitpick, let’s go all in! #StarWarsDebates
